coredata.dc:title: Effects of fluorescent carbon dots from the baked lamb on energy and lipid metabolism
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Lijuan
Na, Xiaokang
Lai, Bin
Song, Yukun
Wang, Haitao
Tan, Mingqian
coredata.dc:description: Food-borne carbon dots (CDs) may cause health risks due to their unique properties. However, previous efforts were mainly focused on the characterization of their physicochemical properties, their effects on cellular metabolism are not entirely revealed. Herein, the features and potential toxicity of CDs from lamb baked for 15, 30, and 45 min were evaluated, their cytotoxicity increased with the extension of baking time. Furthermore, the metabolic responses of PC12 cells after exposure to CDs from lamb baked for 45 min were investigated. The CDs perturbed purine metabolism, causing reactive oxygen species accumulation. Meanwhile, the CDs down-regulated glycolysis and TCA cycle, led to a significant decrease in ATP. Additionally, the CDs induced triglyceride accumulation, mainly through enhanced fatty acid biosynthesis. The adverse effects of CDs from baked lamb involved the perturbation of energy production, purine metabolism, and triglyceride biosynthesis, which provided additional information about the risks of CDs from food items.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Baked lamb
Metabolism
Lipidomics
Cytotoxicity
Reactive oxygen species

Characterization of CDs from baked lamb
Mitochondrial transmembrane potential and ROS assay
Characterization of CDs from baked lamb
Presence and optical properties of CDs from baked lamb
Structure analysis of the CDs from baked lamb
Stability of the CDs from baked lamb
Amino acid metabolism and purine metabolism

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31872915), the Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Liaoning Province (20180540095) and the National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFC1600702).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographs of the aqueous solution of CDs from baked lamb for 15, 30 and 45 min (A–C), respectively, under visible light. Photographs of aqueous solution of CDs separated from baked lamb for 15, 30 and 45 min (D–F), respectively, under UV light. TEM image of CDs from baked lamb for 15, 30 and 45 min (G–I), the inset images illustrate the lattice of the CDs; size distribution histogram and Gauss fitting of particle size of CDs from baked lamb for 15, 30 and 45 min (J–L). Schematic-proposed formation mechanism of the CDs in baked lamb.

Flow cytometry analysis of PC12 cell apoptosis by annexin V-FITC/PI double staining. PC 12 cells without treatment (A) (Negative control). PC12 cells exposed to 0.5 mg mL−1 CDs from baked lamb for 15, 30 and 45 min (B–D). PC12 cells exposed to 4 mg mL−1 CDs from baked lamb for 15, 30 and 45 min (E–G). The relative proportion of apoptosis cells treated with CDs at different concentrations (H). Data were presented as mean ± SD, n = 3 (*, P < 0.05; **, P < 0.01).
Heat map of differential metabolites molecular species in PC 12 cells treated with different doses of CDs (0.5 and 4 mg mL−1) for 24 h compared to the control group.

Disturbance of glycolysis and TCA cycle after exposure to CDs. Red indicates significantly increased metabolites, green indicates significantly decreased metabolites by CDs with different doses. Data were presented as mean ± SD, n = 6 (*, P < 0.05; **, P < 0.01).

Mitochondrial function and glycolysis function in PC 12 cells after 24 h of treatment with CDs (4 mg mL−1) were evaluated using a Seahorse XFe analyzer. Mito Stress Test (A). The basal OCR, proton leak, the maximal respiration, indirectly measured ATP production, and spare respiratory capacity levels are presented (B). Glycolysis Stress Test (C). The glycolysis, glycolytic capacity, glycolytic reserve levels are presented (D). Data were presented as mean ± SD, n = 3 (*, P < 0.05; **, P < 0.01).
Heat map of the differential lipid molecular species in PC 12 cells treated with different doses of CDs (0.5 and 4 mg mL−1) for 24 h compared to the control group.

head.ce:title: Effects of fluorescent carbon dots from the baked lamb on energy and lipid metabolism

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Food-borne carbon dots (CDs) may cause health risks due to their unique properties. However, previous efforts were mainly focused on the characterization of their physicochemical properties, their effects on cellular metabolism are not entirely revealed. Herein, the features and potential toxicity of CDs from lamb baked for 15, 30, and 45 min were evaluated, their cytotoxicity increased with the extension of baking time. Furthermore, the metabolic responses of PC12 cells after exposure to CDs from lamb baked for 45 min were investigated. The CDs perturbed purine metabolism, causing reactive oxygen species accumulation. Meanwhile, the CDs down-regulated glycolysis and TCA cycle, led to a significant decrease in ATP. Additionally, the CDs induced triglyceride accumulation, mainly through enhanced fatty acid biosynthesis. The adverse effects of CDs from baked lamb involved the perturbation of energy production, purine metabolism, and triglyceride biosynthesis, which provided additional information about the risks of CDs from food items.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials have been widely used in the food industry due to their unique physicochemical properties, which significantly improve the food qualities such as extending shelf life, reducing cost, and intensifying nutrition (McClements & Xiao, 2017). The biosafety of nanomaterials has raised great attention in understanding their potential toxicity hazards (Sharifi, Behzadi, Laurent, Forrest, Stroeve, & Mahmoudi, 2012). Several works reported the health risk of nanomaterials present in food items. (Garcia, Shin, & Kim, 2018; Jain, Ranjan, Dasgupta, & Ramalingam, 2018; McClements & Xiao, 2017). Currently, almost all studies regarding nanoparticle safety were focused on artificially synthesized nanoparticles that added to food products. However, the presence of nanostructures in food that formed during food processing was largely ignored, especially if they have potential adverse effects.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of fluorescent nanomaterial which was discovered by Xu et al. (2004). Correlation between food and CDs were established in 2012 since Sk et al. demonstrated the presence of CDs in caramels, and the formation of CDs in food was attributed to the complex reaction among food ingredients during food processing (Sk, Jaiswal, Paul, Ghosh, & Chattopadhyay, 2012). Recently, the universal presence of CDs in food items was proved by a series of reports and their potential adverse effects have been demonstrated (Li et al., 2020; Song et al., 2019). The surface groups on CDs were critical for reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation in the cell, which was regarded as a key factor in CDs induced cytotoxicity (Havrdova et al., 2016). Furthermore, a global metabolite shift was observed after exposure to food-borne CDs (Song, Wang, Zhang, Lai, Liu, & Tan, 2020).

Previously, the presence of CDs in baked lamb was proved and the physicochemical properties, especially the surface groups, were similar to those food-borne CDs that decreased cell viability (Wang et al., 2017). Therefore, it is reasonable to conjecture CDs from baked lamb may disrupt normal metabolic pathways via ROS. Herein, the physicochemical properties of CDs from lamb baked at various time periods were characterized. The metabolomics and lipidomics analyses were performed to investigate the adverse effects induced by CDs from baked lambs. Moreover, the intensity of glycolysis and oxidative phosphorylation after CDs treatment was also monitored by an extracellular flux analyzer. Those results revealed that the CDs-induced cellular disturbances at the molecular level, providing valuable information about the health risk of food-borne CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isolation of CDs from baked lamb
ce:section.ce:para: The lamb was purchased from a supermarket in Dalian, China, which was cut into flakes and baked in an electric oven at 280 °C for 15, 30, and 45 min, respectively. The CDs in baked lamb were isolated according to the previous method with slight modification (Wang et al., 2017). Briefly, the baked lamb was mixed with ethanol and stirred for 24 h. After filtration, the ethanol was removed by vacuum rotary evaporation, the residues that contain CDs was dissolved in ultrapure water. The aqueous solution was dialyzed against deionized water with a membrane of the molecular weight cut off 3500 Da for 3 days after extraction with ethyl acetate. To further purify the CDs, the solution outside dialysis bag was collected and transferred to a new dialysis bag with the molecular weight cut off 500 Da. The solution in this dialysis bag contained CDs and was lyophilized for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs from baked lamb
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology, crystal structure, ultraviolet–visible spectra, fluorescence spectra, Fourier transforms infrared spectra (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), lifetimes and quantum yield (QY) of CDs was determined as reported previously (Wang et al., 2017). The size distribution of CDs was obtained based on the statistical analysis of 100 particles.

Rat pheochromocytoma cells (PC12) were used to assess the cytotoxicity of CDs which were maintained in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) high glucose medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum at 37 °C. The cells were seeded in a 6-well plate and exposed to different dosage of CDs (0.5 and 4 mg mL−1) for 24 h. For apoptosis analysis, the PC12 cells were collected after CDs treatment and stained by annexin V-FITC (fluorescein isothiocyanate)/PI (propidium iodide). For cell cycle analysis, the PC12 cells were fixed by 70% ethanol and then stained with PI. The fluorescence intensity was measured by a FACSVerse flow cytometer (BD Biosciences, San Jose, USA) (Song et al., 2019).

The PC12 cells were seeded in a laser confocal culture dish, after incubating with CDs (4 mg mL−1, produced from lamb after 45 min baking, hereinafter inclusive) for 24 h, they were stained by Nile red according to the previous method (Liu et al., 2015). Lipid droplet was observed by using an SP8 confocal laser fluorescence microscope (Leica, Wetzlar, Germany), the excitation wavelength was 480 nm and the emission wavelength range from 560 to 600 nm.

The PC12 cells were treated with different doses of CDs (0.5 and 4 mg mL−1) for 24 h. The RNA was extracted using a UN1Q-10 column Trizol total RNA extraction kit (Sangon Biotech), and cDNA was synthesized using PrimeScript™ RT reagent Kit (Takara) with gDNA Eraser. RT-qPCR was performed by SYBR® Premix Ex Taq™ II (Tli RNaseH Plus) (Takara) in a Real-Time PCR Thermal Cycler (qTOWER 2.2, Analytikjena, Germany) according to the manufacturers’ instruction. The PCR protocol was carried out as follows: initial denaturation at 95 °C for 30 s, followed by 40 cycles of PCR including denaturation at 95 °C for 5 s, annealing and extension at 60 °C for 30 s, and hold at 95 °C, 55 °C and 95 °C for 15 s . Fold change of mRNA abundance was calculated using 2-ΔΔCt method as described previously (Livak & Schmittgen, 2001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mitochondrial transmembrane potential and ROS assay
ce:section.ce:para: The PC12 cells were seeded into a 12 well plate at a density of 50,000 cells/well. After treating with different concentration of CDs (0.5 and 4 mg mL−1) for 24 h, the mitochondrial transmembrane potential was measured by Mitochondrial Membrane Potential Assay Kits with JC-1 (Beyotime Biotechnology, Shanghai, China) according to the manufacturers’ instruction. Briefly, cells were harvested by trypsin and mixed with 500 μL of working JC-1 staining solution. The cells were collected by centrifugation after incubation in the dark at 37 °C for 20 min, and redundant JC-1 was rinsed with JC-1 staining buffer (4 °C) two times and the cell pellets were re-suspended with 500 μL solution buffer.

Intracellular ROS were detected according to the previous method (Havrdova et al., 2016). PC12 cells were rinsed with PBS and collected by trypsin digestion after incubating with different concentration of CDs (0.5 and 4 mg mL−1) for 2 h. The cell pellets were resuspended in 0.5 mL PBS and stained with 10 μmol L-1 dichloro-dihydro-fluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) for 60 min at 37 °C. The stained cells were collected by centrifugation and resuspend with 0.25 mL PBS. The fluorescent intensity was measured using a fluorescent microplate reader (Infinite F200 PRO, Tican).

The PC12 cells were exposed to different doses of CDs (0.5 and 4 mg mL−1) for 24 h. After incubating with fresh medium for 2 h, the metabolite was extracted. The precooled methanol (80% v/v, −80 °C) was added and incubated for 20 min at −80 °C. After removing the cell fragment by centrifugation, the supernatant was lyophilized and stored at −80 °C for further testing. Detailed conditions for chromatograph and mass spectrometer were described previously (Yuan, Breitkopf, Yang, & Asara, 2012). For lipid extraction, cells were mixed with 300 μL of cold methanol and transferring into a 5 mL tube. The mixture was vortexed for 20 s, before adding1 mL of MTBE. After that, the mixture was vortexed for 5 min. Subsequently, 250 μL of H2O was added to form phase separation. After equilibration at 4 °C for 10 min, the mixture was centrifuged at 4000 g for 5 min at 4 °C. The upper layer was collected and dried with nitrogen atmosphere for LC-MS analysis. Detailed conditions for chromatograph and mass spectrometer were described previously (Xuan et al., 2018).

The PC12 cells were seeded on XF 8 well plates (10,000 cells/well) and treated with CDs (4 mg mL−1) for 24 h. The hydration probe was carried out before the test and placed in a 37 °C incubator for 8 h. Glycolysis stress and mitochondrial stress test were performed according to manufacturer’s instruments by using an Agilent Technologies Seahorse XF Analyzer (Seahorse Bioscience, Billerica, MA, USA).

The results were expressed as means ± standard deviation (SD). Student’s t test was performed by statistical product and service solutions (SPSS) statistics 19 package (IBM, USA), statistically significant differences were reported if p < 0.05. For metabolomic analysis, the R programming language (version 3.6.3) was employed to perform t-test, both p and false discovery rate (FDR) values of < 0.05 were considered significantly different from the control. The heatmap was obtained by software TBtools 6.2. Partial least squares discriminate analysis (PLS-DA) was carry out by SIMCA-P11.5 (Umetrics, Umeå, Sweden).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs from baked lamb
Presence and optical properties of CDs from baked lamb
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs in lamb baked under 280 °C for different times (15, 30, and 45 min) were extracted and labeled as CD-1, CD-2, and CD-3, respectively. The extracted CDs dispersed well in water, and their solution turned brown with the extension of baking time (Fig. 1 A-C). The absorption band in the region of 200–250 nm in UV–vis spectra may be attributed to π-π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains (Fig. S1), which typically does not generate fluorescence (Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2016; Dong et al., 2013). In contrast, the increased absorption for CD-2 and CD-3 at around 320 nm was associated with surface states that determined their fluorescence properties (Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2016; Dong et al., 2013; Li et al., 2018). Correspondingly, the CD-3 exhibited the most robust blue fluorescence under UV excitation (Fig. 1D–F). These results were further supported by QYs analysis since the QYs values of CD-1, CD-2, and CD-3 were 2.93%, 10.89% and 13.97%, respectively (Tables S1–3). The CDs displayed analogous fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. S1), a typical bathochromic phenomenon in CDs were observed, and the maximum excitation and emission wavelength of CD-1, CD-2, and CD-3 were 360/430, 400/455 and 400/455 nm, respectively. The lifetime measured at room temperature was 6.70, 7.89 and 7.96 ns, respectively (Fig. S2), with the decay curves fitted with a three-exponential decay function. Multiple lifetimes may be attributed to diverse fluorophores or energy levels and agreed with the excitation dependent emission. Notably, lifetime increased with baking time, which suggested the surface states changed during baking.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure analysis of the CDs from baked lamb
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of CDs from baked lambs characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 1G–I) demonstrated that all the CDs obtained from the different baking time were spherical in sharp and well dispersed. Their average diameters tended to decrease as the extension of the baking time. The average size of CD-1, CD-2, and CD-3 were 4.1, 3.7, and 2.6 nm, respectively (Fig. 1J–K), and cross-checked by atomic force microscopy (AFM) as well (Fig. S3). The results suggested the compounds in lambs generated large nanostructures initially and then broken down into small CDs along with an extension of baking time (Cartoon in Fig. 1). Similar results were also obtained recently (Song et al., 2019). Furthermore, carbon nanocrystals were found in the CDs by high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (Fig. 1G-I, insets). Interplanar spacings of CD-1, CD-2, and CD-3 were 0.26, 0.21, and 0.21 nm, which were corresponded to the (020), (100), and (100) facet of graphite (Nie et al., 2014). The CDs obtained at different baking times shown similar X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a broad peak centered at around 23° (Fig. S4). Integrated results from XRD and HRTEM suggested the core of those CDs was crystalline while their surface was amorphous structure (X. Chen, Jin, Wu, Tung, & Tang, 2014).

FTIR spectra analysis revealed the existence of –OH or –NH2 (3300 cm−1), –CH (2963 cm−1), –CO (1670 cm−1), –CS– (1160 cm−1) and –COC– (1048 cm−1) on the surface of CDs (Fig. S5) (X. Chen, Jin, Wu, Tung, & Tang, 2014; Wang, Lu, Hou, Liu, & Zhang, 2016). Compared with CD-1, the peak at 1048 cm−1 decreased significantly in CD-2 and CD-3, indicating decomposing of COC if prolong the baking time. The surface groups of CDs were further analyzed by XPS. (Fig. S5). The four major peaks at 168, 285, 400 and 532 eV in XPS survey spectra of CDs indicated the presence of S, C, N, and O, respectively. The corresponding elemental composition of the samples was listed in Table S4. The content of C and S increased gradually with the extension of the baking time, while the content of O decreased slightly. Different from C, O, and S, the relative content of N was almost constant during the baking process. This result revealed that carbonization degree of CDs from baked lamb increased with the extension of baking time. Besides, the high-resolution XPS spectra were performed (Fig. S6–8). The presence of CC, CN, CO, and CO was confirmed by C1s XPS spectra since their characteristic peaks at 284.5, 285.5, 286.4, 288.6 eV were observed, respectively (Jiang et al., 2015; J. Wang, Wang, & Chen, 2012). Peaks at 399.2, 399.9 and 400.8 eV in the high-resolution N1s spectra could be attributed to pyridine N, amide N and NH Peak (Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2016). The binding energy of *OC–O, COC, and OC–O* was 531.4, 532.3 and 533.2 eV, respectively, which were detected in the high-resolution spectra of O1s (Chen, Jin, Wu, Tung, & Tang, 2014; Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2016). The high-resolution spectra of S2P suggested the presence of C-SO2, C-SO3, and C-SO4 (Wang, Lu, Hou, Liu, & Zhang, 2016). Furthermore, the relative intensity at 532.2 eV decreased in CD-2 and CD-3 compared with CD-1, revealing the corresponding decrease of COC in those CDs, which was consistent with FTIR. In summary, the presence of –NH2, –OH, –COOH, and –CS– on the surface of CDs was confirmed by XPS and FTIR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the CDs from baked lamb
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of pH, continuous irradiation and ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of CDs were investigated (Fig. S9). The variation of fluorescence intensity of CDs was<18% over a wide range of pH (2–11). The pH value of solutions may affect the dissociation equilibrium of surface groups and thus change their fluorescence intensity. Therefore, this result suggested surface groups of those CDs were relatively stable under different pH environment. The fluorescent intensity of CDs was almost unaffected by ionic strength even the NaCl concentration was up to 0.25 mol L−1. These results further suggested their excellent salt tolerance nature. Moreover, only neglected decrease of fluorescence was observed under continuous light exposition. In brief, those results indicated that fluorescence of CDs was relatively stable under various conditions.

Materials in nano-size could induce necrosis or apoptosis as reported previously (Mohammadinejad et al., 2019). Therefore, evaluation of the potential adverse effects of CDs from baked lamb was important. Annexin V-FITC/PI double staining tests were performed to examine the cytotoxicity of CDs obtained from baked lambs with various baking time (Fig. 2 ). The results indicated that cytotoxicity of those CDs was relatively low at low concentration (0.5 mg mL−1) since more than 90% cells were alive after incubation for 24 h. Moreover, dose-dependent cytotoxicity was observed. The percentage of live cells decreased along with the increase of CDs concentration. Notably, the cytotoxicity of CDs from longer baking time was much higher than that obtained from shorter ones. The ratio of live cells after treatment with 4 mg mL-1CD-1 was 95%, which was comparable to that in the control group. On the contrary, CD-2, and CD-3 led to a considerable decrease in the proportion of living cells at the same concentration, which decreased to 74% and 45% respectively. Furthermore, the percentage of necrosis increased correspondingly under the same conditions. The effects of CDs from the baked lamb on the cell cycle were further investigated by flow cytometry to provide deep understandings of their cytotoxicity. Generally, the cell cycle could be divided into three periods, namely G0/G1 phase (quiescence/gap 1), S phase (DNA synthesis), and G2/M phase (gap 2 and mitosis). However, the cell cycle will be delayed if DNA is damaged (Mohammadinejad et al., 2019). In many cases, nanomaterials lead to cell cycle arrest in different phase (Huang et al., 2018), so we deduced that the CDs from baked lamb may cause cell cycle arrest as well. Apparent changes in cell cycle destructions occurred in PC12 after incubation with CD-1, CD-2, and CD-3 at a different concentration as shown in Fig. S10. Furthermore, the cell cycle exhibited a dose-dependent manner. Populations of G2/M phases of PC12 cells increased as the raise of CDs concentration, and the percentages increased from 22% to 33%, from 25% to 35%, and from 24% to 40%, respectively, after treating with CD-1, CD-2, and CD-3. Likewise, the proportion of cells in the G1/G0 phase decreased correspondingly. Moreover, the most significant changes in the cell cycle were found when treated with CD-3. This result was in line with that from cell apoptosis analysis. In brief, those results suggested that the CDs from baked lamb that baked for a longer time were more toxic. Similar results were obtained in grilled hamburger in which the cytotoxicity intended to increase when the grill temperature increased (Li et al., 2017). The higher cytotoxicity of those CDs could be associated with carbonization degree and surface groups (Havrdova et al., 2016).

In order to gain an in-depth understanding of the potential adverse effects of CDs from baked lamb, the most toxic CD-3 was selected as an example, and the variation of metabolites and lipid profile after incubating with CDs were investigated subsequently by the targeted metabolomics and lipidomics methods. Partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) was applied to visualize the difference among CDs treated groups and the control groups (Song, Wang, Zhang, Lai, Liu, & Tan, 2020). The PLS-DA score plot shows that the CDs treated groups and control groups were clearly separated without overlapping (Fig. S11). The cumulative predictive capacity (Q2) and faithful representation (R2Y) of PLS-DA for metabolome were 0.964 and 0.989, respectively. Along the component 1 direction, the CDs treated groups were separated from control groups. The 0.5 mg mL−1 of CDs–treated groups were separated from the 4 mg mL−1 of CDs–treated group along the component 2 direction completely. Similar to PLS-DA score plot for metabolomics, the CDs-treated cells were distinctly separated from control group when using lipidomics data to construct PLS-DA model. The cumulative predictive capacity and faithful representation of PLS-DA for lipidomics were 0.918 and 0.986, respectively. These results revealed that the CD treatments led to significant alternations in metabolites and lipids of the testing cells (Zhang, Shao, Zhao, Li, & Cai, 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amino acid metabolism and purine metabolism
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs treatment led to a significant disorder of amino acid in PC12 (Fig. S12). Compared to the control groups, the amino acid levels of lysine, histidine, glutamine, arginine, phenylalanine, tyrosine, proline, valine, serine, asparagine, methionine, cysteine, threonine in the cell that was treated with CDs trended to be increased (Fig. 3 ), with exceptions of aspartate, which decreased at a high dose of CDs. This result implied that amino acid metabolism was enhanced after incubating with CDs. Furthermore, in comparison with the CDs treated groups and control groups, the level of arginine, ornithine, and citrulline, which involved in the urea cycle in PC12 cells, increased significantly. This result suggested the urea cycle was up-regulated after exposed to CDs. Urea cycle was critical for ammonia removing, toxic ammonia generated during oxidative deamination of amino acids were converted into urea through the urea cycle (Geng et al., 2015). It is essential to mention that the N-acetyl-glutamate level increased in the presence of CDs. N-acetyl-glutamate is an activator of carbamoyl phosphate synthetase Ⅰ (rate-limiting enzyme of the urea cycle). Amino acid decomposition lead to excessive ammonia production in the cell, which activates the synthesis of N-acetyl-glutamate (Zhang, Shao, Zhao, Li, & Cai, 2019). In turn, by activating carbamoyl phosphate synthetase Ⅰ, the ammonia will be converted to urea for excretion (Fig. S12). Therefore, the accumulation of N-acetyl-glutamate provided additional evidence about the activation of the urea cycle. Moreover, the upregulation of the urea cycle was demonstrated directly through the quantification of urea level before and after CDs treatment, the fold change of urea after low-dose and high-dose CDs treatment was 2.0 and 1.57, respectively. This result suggested the CDs exposure improved amino acid metabolism in PC12 cell.

The CDs exposure induced a significant disorder of purine metabolism in PC12 cells. In CD-treated cells, key intermediate involved in purine metabolism increased obviously (Fig. 3), indicating the upregulation of this metabolic pathway. It is notable that hypoxanthine could be converted into xanthine by xanthine oxidase, which produces hydrogen peroxide (Zhang, Shao, Zhao, Li, & Cai, 2019). Thus, it is reasonable to infer that the activation of purine metabolism, especially the elevation of hypoxanthine suggested purine metabolism may be a critical source for ROS production in PC12 cell after CDs treatment.

The results from metabolomics analysis revealed that glycolysis and the TCA cycle were perturbed after CD exposure (Fig. 4 ). Eleven metabolites related to glycolysis and TCA cycle, including fructose-1,6-bisphosphate, glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate, dihydroxyacetone phosphate, 1,3-diphosphate glycerate, 3-phosphoglycerate, phosphoenolpyruvate, pyruvate, thiamine pyrophosphate, isocitrate, succinate, and fumarate, were detected. The metabolites were almost unchanged under low-dose CDs, except fructose-1,6-bisphosphate increased about 2.5 times. However, the relative content of metabolites involved in glycolysis and the TCA cycle changed significantly after incubation with high-dose CDs. All the metabolites in glycolysis were upregulated, while succinate and fumarate, which were involved in the TCA cycle, were downregulated. It is important to note the decrease of thiamine pyrophosphate, a cofactor for key enzymes in the TCA cycle, such as α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase and pyruvate dehydrogenase, demonstrating downregulation of the TCA cycle under high-dose CDs (Bubber, Ke, & Gibson, 2004). Furthermore, the inhibition of the TCA cycle may result in the accumulation of glycolysis intermediate as well.

To further confirm the adverse effects of CDs on energy metabolism, glycolysis and mitochondrial respiration intensity were measured by extracellular flux analyzer after treated with high-dose CDs. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the oxygen consumption rate (OCR) was determined to evaluate mitochondrial respiration, which reflected the intensity of the TCA cycle. The OCR decreased after oligomycin addition since oxidative phosphorylation was inhibited. The difference of OCR before and after oligomycin addition indicated ATP production. ATP production after CD treatment was only about 25% compared to the control group. Upon the addition of FCCP (uncoupling agent), the OCR reached its maximum, which represented the maximal respiration of the cell. Subsequently, the basal respiration of the cell was measured by adding antimycin A and rotenone. The results revealed that the CD treatment had a significant impact on both basal and maximal respiration. The basal respiration in CD-treated groups was about 50% of that in the control group, and the maximal respiration decreased from 40 to 15 pmol min−1 after CDs incubation. The extracellular acidification rate (ECAR) represents glycolysis intensity. Similar to OCR, the ECAR value in CD-treated cells was significantly lower than that of standard culture conditions (Fig. 5C). The sharp decrease in glycolytic capacity after CD exposure provided substantial evidence that high dose CDs inhibited glycolysis. In summary, results from metabolites and extracellular flux analyzer indicated that energy metabolism was disrupted by CDs at relatively high concentration.

The CDs from other food items affected energy metabolism were also reported. The glycolytic capacity decreased significantly after the addition of CDs from roasted Atlantic salmon (Song, Wang, Zhang, Lai, Liu, & Tan, 2020; Song et al., 2019). Moreover, a similar result was also obtained as the CDs from canned yellow croaker down-regulated the glycolytic pathway (Li et al., 2019). The CDs from canned yellow croaker inhibited the activities of hexokinase and pyruvate kinase, which were the key enzymes involved in glycolysis. Therefore, the interruption of energy metabolism may be a key mechanism involved in food-borne CDs induced toxicity.

The effect of CDs on lipid metabolism was investigated to further understand their potential toxicity (Fig. S13). As shown in Fig. 6 , several classes of lipids were significantly altered, including sphingolipids, plasmalogens, hexaglycosylceramides, glycerophospholipids, and glycerolipids. The changes suggested that the CDs disrupted biosynthesis and degradation of glycerophospholipids and glycerolipids; fatty acid metabolism, and sphingomyelinceramide (SM-Cer) signaling pathway.

Phospholipids are vital compounds that involve the formation of the cellular membrane. Upon exposure to CDs, the level of phospholipids was altered significantly (Fig. 6), indicating the homeostasis of the cell membrane was disrupted (Zhang, Shao, Zhao, Li, & Cai, 2019). Notably, PG is a precursor for cardiolipin synthesis, which is a critical compound in the mitochondrial membrane. The remarkable variation of PG indicating CDs exposure causes mitochondrial membrane damage as described previously (Chen et al., 2018). To further evaluate the integrity of the mitochondrial membrane, JC-1 was used to detect mitochondrial membrane potential changes before and after CDs addition (Fig. S14). The fluorescence intensity ratio between 595 nm and 535 nm decreased with the increase of CDs concentration., suggesting mitochondrial membrane damage occurred (Li et al., 2020).

The conversion of SM to Cer played a critical role in cell apoptosis (Zhang, Shao, Zhao, Li, & Cai, 2019). In this study, all the detected species of SMs showed a decreasing tendency in the CDs treatment groups, while 1.08–2.53 times increase of the abundance of some Cer species was determined (Fig. 6), suggesting cell apoptosis signal were activated and was coincided with the results obtained from flow cytometer. Moreover, high Cer was also associated with oxidative stress since the ROS production can be enhanced by Cer through disrupting the mitochondrial respiratory electron chain (Andrieu-Abadie, Gouaze, Salvayre, & Levade, 2001). To further verify the elevation of cellular ROS level, the production of ROS was detected by the fluorescent probe DCFH-DA. As shown in Fig. S15, the fluorescence intensity increased significantly after exposure to CDs compared with the control group, demonstrating that the CDs from baked lamb induced the cellular ROS accumulation and may lead to oxidative stress in PC12 cells.

Plasmalogens are ether-containing glycerophospholipids, which present a vinyl-ether band at the sn-1 position. It was reported that plasmalogens could act as antioxidants since they could reactive with free radicals efficiently (Lopez et al., 2018). The decreasing trend of plasmalogens (PE-O and PC-O) was observed in the cell after treating with CDs (Fig. 6). This result suggested oxidative stress increased in CDs treated cells. The decrease in PC-O was also observed in rate after treated with ZnO nanoparticles, which can be attributed to scavenging free radicals generated by ZnO nanoparticles (Lee et al., 2018).

The CDs exposure led to TAG accumulation in PC12 cells as all the TAG species increased significantly (Fig. 6). This result was further confirmed by confocal laser scanning microscope. Nile red is a fluorescence dye that emits strong fluorescence in nonpolar environments. Lipid droplet, which storage TAG in the cell, could be visualized when stained by Nile red (Greenspan, Mayer, & Fowler, 1985). Both the size and number of lipid droplets were increased after treating with CDs (Fig. S16), further supported the results from lipidomics. The elevated ROS level was closely associated with TAG accumulation. For example, oxidative stress has been demonstrated as a critical factor that triggers TAG accumulation in hepatocytes (Sekiya, Hiraishi, Touyama, & Sakamoto, 2008). Furthermore, the ROS-activated pathway has been proved involving in TAG accumulation in glia (Liu et al., 2015). Moreover, nanoparticles triggered TAG accumulation was related to ROS as well (Przybytkowski, Behrendt, Dubois, & Maysinger, 2009). In this study, CDs exposure leading to an increased ROS as described above, therefore the accumulation in TAG might be related to CDs induced ROS generation.

It is reported that SREBP (sterol regulatory element-binding protein), a transcription factor that controls fatty acid biosynthesis, can be active by ROS and thus lead to TAG accumulation (Sekiya, Hiraishi, Touyama, & Sakamoto, 2008). To further investigate the effect of CDs on lipid metabolism, the mRNA abundance of SREBP and the other genes related to lipid biosynthesis and metabolism were detected (Fig. S17). The result reveals that mRNA level of SREBP increased with the CDs concentration in a dose-dependent manner. Moreover, the mRNA level of SREBP target genes involved in the fatty acid synthesis, including ATP citrate lyase (ACL), acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC), fatty acid synthase (FAS) and stearoyl-CoA desaturase 1 (SCD1), (Horton et al., 2003) increased significantly after CDs exposure. Citrate synthase (CS) was critical in transport intramitochondrial acetyl-CoA into the cytoplasm for fatty acid synthesis (Luengo, Gui, & Vander Heiden, 2017), while diacylglycerol acyltransferase (DGAT) catalyzes the last step for TAG synthesis. (Coleman & Lee, 2004)Those two genes were critical for fatty acid synthesis as well. The mRNA level of genes coding these two enzymes were upregulated after CDs treatment as expected.

Moreover, the variation of metabolites involved in TAG synthesis was agreed with the mRNA level. Free fatty acid (FFA), the building blocks for TAG synthesis, increased after CDs incubation. DAG was the direct substrate for TAG synthesis and the conversion of DAG to TAG was the committed step for TAG synthesis. The entire DAG level exhibit a small but remarkable elevation after CDs treatment although some DAG species decreased. The increased FFA and DAG level (Fig. 6) after CDs treatment provided further evidence about TAG accumulation in the presence of CDs. Those results suggested ROS related SREBP activation played an essential role in TAG accumulation in PC12 cells after CDs treatment.

This study provided primary understandings about properties and potential adverse effects of CDs from baked lambs via the metabolomics and lipidomics approach. Baking time played an important role in determining the particle size, surface groups and optical properties of the CDs. Exposure to CDs led to a toxic effect on PC12 cells as the cell viability was reduced and metabolite profile was disrupted. Similar to other nanomaterials, CDs could generate ROS in the cell which resulted in oxidative stress. The imbalance of redox status disrupted energy metabolism, amino acid metabolism, purine metabolism, and lipid metabolism. Furthermore, the metabolism disorder resulted in mitochondrial dysfunction and up-regulation of purine metabolism, thus enhancing ROS production and exacerbating CD damage.

Lijuan Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xiaokang Na: Data curation. Bin Lai: Writing - review & editing. Yukun Song: Methodology. Haitao Wang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Investigation. Mingqian Tan: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31872915), the Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Liaoning Province (20180540095) and the National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFC1600702).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.127832.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Yuan et al., 2012

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A positive/negative ion-switching, targeted mass spectrometry-based metabolomics platform for bodily fluids, cells, and fresh and fixed tissue
Nature Protocols

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yuan, M., Breitkopf, S. B., Yang, X., & Asara, J. M. (2012). A positive/negative ion-switching, targeted mass spectrometry-based metabolomics platform for bodily fluids, cells, and fresh and fixed tissue. Nature Protocols, 7(5), 872-881. https://doi.org/10.1038/nprot.2012.024.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Integration of metabolomics and lipidomics reveals metabolic mechanisms of triclosan-induced toxicity in human hepatocytes
Environmental Science & Technology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, H., Shao, X., Zhao, H., Li, X., & Cai, Z. (2019). Integration of metabolomics and lipidomics reveals metabolic mechanisms of triclosan-induced toxicity in human hepatocytes. Environmental Science & Technology, 53(9), 5406-5415. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.est.8b07281.
